sourceMovie.title: Open Range
sourceMovie.whyPeopleLoveIt: Audiences latched onto Open Range (2003) as a western theatrical experience: big enough for a group watch, specific enough to reward a second viewing once you know where it's headed. The western and drama blend is the engine: it lets the film shift registers without feeling like two different movies stitched together.

recommendations.title: The Quick and the Dead
recommendations.whyYoullLoveIt: If Open Range clicked for you, The Quick and the Dead (1995) is a strong next stop: both trade in western and drama, with emotional whiplash that feels honest. Expect a different story, but a similar willingness to go big when the moment demands it.

recommendations.title: Silverado
recommendations.whyYoullLoveIt: If Open Range clicked for you, Silverado (1985) is a strong next stop: both trade in western and drama, with momentum and clean stakes. Expect a different story, but a similar willingness to go big when the moment demands it.

faq.question: What should I watch after Open Range?
faq.answer: Try The Quick and the Dead, Silverado, Forsaken—each shares DNA with Open Range while changing the setting enough to feel fresh.

faq.question: What genre is Open Range?
faq.answer: Open Range (2003) is best described as Western, Drama, Action. Stores and algorithms use different labels, but that mix is what fans actually recognize on a rewatch.

faq.question: Is Open Range worth revisiting?
faq.answer: Viewer scores sit around 7.2/10 on TMDB—high enough that replays are common for people who like this western lane.
